name: Detroit (United States)

colors.symbolism: the lower-hoist quarter — the field of France's fleurs-de-lis
the fleurs-de-lis of France and the three lions of England
the upper-hoist quarter — field for the three lions of England
the cantons / chiefs of the United States quarters

description: A flag quartered to commemorate the four powers that have governed Detroit. Upper hoist: a red field with three gold lions passant guardant (England). Lower hoist: a white field with five gold fleurs-de-lis (France). Upper fly: thirteen blue and white stripes with thirteen white stars on a blue chief (the United States, in its 1776 form). Lower fly: thirteen red and white stripes with a navy canton bearing thirteen stars (the United States, in another arrangement). At the centre is a circular seal depicting two figures — one mourning the burning city, one consoling — with the mottoes "SPERAMUS MELIORA" ("We hope for better things") and "RESVRGET CINERIBVS" ("It will rise from the ashes"), commemorating the great fire of 1805.
history: Designed in 1907 by attorney and civic leader David E. Heineman, first flown on 12 June 1908 at a Detroit Tigers celebration, but not officially adopted until 1948 under Mayor Eugene Van Antwerp after an outside firm requested an official flag and the oversight came to light. The Latin mottoes "Speramus Meliora" and "Resurget Cineribus" were composed by Father Gabriel Richard following the great fire that destroyed Detroit on 11 June 1805. The design has been refined several times, most recently in 2000.

features.symbolism: the four quarters commemorate the four nations that have governed Detroit since its founding by Antoine de la Mothe Cadillac in 1701: France (lower hoist), England (upper hoist) and the United States (upper and lower fly, in two flag forms)

features.role: three gold lions of England (passant guardant)
features.symbolism: the royal arms of England, recalling Detroit's period under British rule from 1760 (capture during the French and Indian War) until the Jay Treaty implementation in 1796
the royal arms of France, recalling Detroit's foundation by Antoine de la Mothe Cadillac in 1701 and its period as a French colonial outpost (Fort Pontchartrain du Détroit)
thirteen red and white stripes recalling the original thirteen colonies / states of the United States, marking the city's period under American rule from 1796
thirteen white stars on the lower-fly canton, again representing the original thirteen states of the Union

features.role: seal of the City of Detroit
features.symbolism: the seal at the centre depicts two figures, one mourning over the ruins of the burning city, the other consoling and pointing to a brighter future; bears the mottoes "Speramus Meliora" ("We hope for better things") and "Resurget Cineribus" ("It shall rise from the ashes"), composed by Father Gabriel Richard following the great fire of 11 June 1805
